I am curious about the place where you g: I was born in NYC but grew up in Kingston, NY, where I went to school all my life. My high school class is around 300 students. It's a nice place to grow up. I heard Kingston was a major manufacturing center for IBM mainframe. I guess it's a normal middle class town. You can google for the exact demographic breakdown. At school, most kids are white, some Asians, less than 10%.
Can you describe your family you grew up: My parents came to the U.S, 30 years ago and attended colleges here. They met and got married. My grandma owns a Chinese restaurant and my parents went into this family business. I am the middle child of three children. I have an older brother who is a senior at Princeton University, and a younger brother who is in middle school.
Most memorable life experience: I learned to play marimba during Covid lockdown. I have been taking piano lesson since I was young. My parents wanted me to learn it. I also learned to play violin and I am the concert master for my school orchestra. I wanted to learn something new while locked down in my house, so I asked the band leader to teach me marimba because it has keys that are like piano so it's easy for me to learn. Many of my friends are in the band so I get to play with them during marching band performances. I really like that. I now teach marimba to the underclass kids, just like how the upper classmate who taught me few years ago. Learning to play marimba is something I decided myself because I wanted to learn it. I like computer so I learned to program Python on my own by watching the videos on Youtube. I believe technology can help mankind to live in a better world. MIT is the top college for someone like me who want to learn and use technology.
Accomplishment: I learned to program on my own during high school freshman year. I also learned to build a simple App in iOS in Swift. I wrote an App to keep track of my restaurant reviews of local restaurants that I have dined. If you want to see, I can show it to you. It's on my phone. One challenge is learning the programming language syntax and understand the library packages. I watched a lot of Youtube videos that teach how to do these, and I did many trial and errors to experiment and learn. It took a while for me to get the App to run without bugs. It wasn't easy to write a working App but I was able to figure it out.
Hobbies: I like to read sci-fi books. I like Asimov robot series. I bought the Three-Body Problem book but I have not start reading it yet. I read few hours a week. Senior year has been very busy with school work, MITES, orchestra practice, marching band practice, and college applications, I don't have much time this semester. I am hoping to have more down time next semester. I have been reading sci-fi since freshman year high school. I like the future that the author imagined.
Motivations + Drives: Robot is interesting. It's like a creation that is an image of ourselves. It can help us doing laborious work; it can be our companion.
Time management: I plan my activities using the calendar app on my phone. Most of the school activities are known and I just have to know to avoid schedule conflicts. When I see a block of time that is not busy, I would find a quiet corner to read my book or play a video game on my phone.
Biggest challenge: There so many things that I want to do and pursue. The biggest challenge is to choose a few of them and focus on doing them well. Staying focus is difficult in our world with so many distractions.
Taking risks: When I decided to learn about marimba over Zoom, my parents weren't happy, because they think I was wasting my time, that it's not necessary as I was playing in orchestra already. They also think playing in the band is not as challenging compare to playing in the orchestra. Doing something new with low reward doesn't make sense to them. I wanted to prove to them that I can pursue my interest without impacting my academic performance.
Teamwork and leadership: I prefer working on a team, like how I worked with MITES students from across the country. For example, I was accepted into MITES program at MIT for high school students. I chose to learn about quantum computing. During the first lecture, the lecturer went over linear algebra with the class but most of us never took linear algebra so we were totally lost. However, after the class, we created a discussion group on the Discord server and over the week, we helped each other to learn about linear algebra and other topics that we didn't understand. I really like the comradeship and collaboration among my fellow classmates. I build a pseudorandom number generator using 4 qubits, first simulated on a computer, then I ran it on IBM Quantum Computer Cloud. It worked. I truly enjoy working together with other students because it is a much faster way to learn and to accomplish the project goal. In the MITES program, we help each other with problem sets. We would work on the problem individually, but when we encounter problem in doing the homework, we would ask other for help, to get suggestion on how to overcome the problem. We don't copy each other's answer. When we work on a problem sets together, we don't always agree on the right approach to solve the problem. So, we each pursue our own approach and compare the answer to determine who has the better approach.  We are aware that in the real world, there maybe multiple solutions to the same problem and the best solution is the one that satisfy the requirement the best.
What's your biggest weakness?: Sometime my curiosity would get the better of me, that I get side tracked by something that caught my interest. I was researching on quantum computing by reading the Wikipedia pages. I clicked on some of the reference links and read those pages that have more reference links. Next thing I know, I was reading about 16th century mathematicians.  I find them fascinating to live in that period of time and able to think about problems that we are trying to solve 400 years later.
Why MIT: I heard MIT has many top technical students from around the world. I believe I will get the most out of my college education by working with them and learning from them.
Preferred MIT activities: I would like to work on a research project for a professor and his team, so I can learn from them.
career aspirations and MIT: I am interested in machine learning and AI; I would like to use them to improve human health, perhaps in drug discovery.
Asked FAQs: I will tell you if you tell me your strength first.

This was the first time I ever failed any test. I remember telling myself there is no need to panic or feel discouraged and think I may not do well in this course. I just need to find more time to make sure I fullly understand the concepts, especially the parts I got confused with. In the end, I got A for this course. What I learned from this experience is that when you found something not working, of course you need to make some changes to address the underlying causes (e.g. time management, clarify confusion to get a better understanding), but at the same time you need to remain calm and level-headed, not to overreact on the situation or make dramatic change to your learning strategy or routine. The ability to analyze a situation and find out the real root cause and know the right way to address the issue is universally applicable to many problems and subjects.
Taking risks: I don't know if I would call it risk, but I decided not to change the way I study when I failed the calculus test. I believe that at a high level my study methods and strategies should be applicable to all the subjects. Different people learn differently. I just try to find what works for me and stick to it
Teamwork and leadership: I have had some projects for which I worked by myself, and some other projects where I worked on a team. So I think it depends on the type of problem and project. I feel having a team member who seemingly didn't contribute was an example of a conflict because other team members didn't think it is fair for this person to take credit for the project and started to complain that they simply did too much. After we got feedback from this person and created a plan with some tasks he could do, everyone was more satisfied. I worked on a project where a team of 4 members needed to create an online barter system. We spent a lot of time brainstorming the business process and the system flow at the beginning, and then mapped our ideas to system design. Because different members have different schedules, I was the coordinator to schedule regular meetings among us, create the meeting agendas, documenting discussions, monitoring our progress to make sure we met the milestones at different stages, etc.
Biggest strength: I would think I tend to remain calm when something bad happens and my mind immediately starts to analyze the situation to find the quick/best way to address the issue at hand instead of trying to point fingers and find excuses for what happened. What happened is already in the past so there is no point to dwell on it. What's more important is to learn from past mistakes and do better in the future. There was once a project where we built a structure with cardboards and stuff. We worked on it for multiple days. Right before the submission deadline, the structure was accidentally damaged (it was fragile) and a core piece broke. We didn't have time to get the replacement. Some members of the team started to blame on the person who caused the accident while I was studying the broken piece and thinking what else we could use to create a "make-shift" part that may function. I shared my thoughts and convinced the team members to work together to fix the structure.
What's your biggest weakness?: I am not good at directions and the spacial aspect. I often become disoriented in a new physical setting. When I went to a new building, I had trouble remembering how I got to a specific office, especially when someone led me there, so I didn't know the way back.
Why MIT: I feel MIT is the best at developing new technologies and applying these technologies to real-world problems, which is what I am most interested in doing.
Preferred MIT activities: Some pilot applications of the cutting edge technologies to help people in need would be great. I hope the school can provide programs or opportunities like this so students can apply what they have learned to build something and easily find real users to help test and iteratively improve it
career aspirations and MIT: I want to become a scientist/engineer who can develop new technologies and new applications of technolgies to benefit people in need. MIT would give me the opportunities to learn from the best and work with the best to achieve that goal
